meta.purpose: Reasoning chains for why a motion decision is right or wrong. These are the logical scaffolds agents use to justify choices under uncertainty.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user asks why, tradeoffs, diagnosis, quality review, or strategic choice.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Choose attention path before choosing animation effects.
logic: Effects are tools, not strategy.
Viewers process hierarchy before appreciating detail.
Complex motion without hierarchy creates cognitive load.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Ask what the viewer should notice first, second, and never.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Perceived quality increases as visible motion decreases — up to a point of lifelessness, which is far harder to reach than most assume.
logic: Over-animated interfaces read as cheap because cheap products overcompensate with decoration.
Premium brands (Apple, Stripe, Linear) use sparse, precise motion — few elements move, but each movement has clear intent.
The human perceptual system interprets 'lots of things moving' as chaos, not sophistication.
Removing 30% of motion almost always increases perceived quality. Removing 60% often reaches the sweet spot.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Before shipping any animation, delete the two most decorative motion groups and re-evaluate. If the communication is clearer, ship the reduced version. The default position should be 'prove why this motion is necessary' — not 'prove why we should remove it.'

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Easing choice is an emotional decision, not a technical default. The wrong easing undermines the entire communication.
logic: Easing conveys weight, confidence, playfulness, urgency, or calm — it is the emotional grammar of motion.
Bounce and elastic easing on enterprise products reads as unprofessional because bounces signal 'fun toy,' not 'reliable tool.'
Linear easing reads as mechanical and cheap because it lacks the acceleration/deceleration of real-world physics.
Power-out easing (slow start, fast finish) reads as confident — the element commits to its destination.
Agents default to power2.inOut because it's a safe middle ground, but safe middle-ground motion produces generic, AI-looking results.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Before choosing an easing curve, name the emotional word the motion should convey: confident, playful, urgent, calm, heavy, light. Then pick the easing that matches. Power-out for confidence. Elastic for playfulness (consumer only). Power-in for heaviness. Never use the default without stating the emotional intent first.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: The sequence of motion events matters more than the visual novelty of any single effect.
logic: A well-sequenced scene with simple fades and slides will outperform a poorly-sequenced scene with elaborate effects.
Choreography = the story the motion tells. Effects = the visual vocabulary it uses.
Agents tend to focus on effects (glow, blur, 3D rotation) because effects are visually novel. But the viewer experiences choreography (what happens when) as the primary narrative.
Bad choreography with great effects is still confusing. Good choreography with simple effects reads as elegant.
reasoning_chains.so_what: When reviewing or generating animation, evaluate the sequence first: what happens at 0ms, 300ms, 600ms, 1000ms? Is there a clear subject evolving through time? Only after the sequence works should you consider whether an effect (glow, mask, parallax) adds or distracts.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: The human visual system can track approximately 1-3 distinct motion groups at once. Exceeding this threshold degrades perceived quality regardless of technical execution.
logic: Cognitive psychology research on multiple object tracking (MOT) shows humans max out at 3-4 tracked objects in controlled lab conditions.
In a real browsing context — where the viewer is also reading, deciding, and navigating — the effective motion tracking limit is closer to 1-2.
Every motion group beyond the tracking limit creates cognitive noise. The viewer stops tracking specifics and perceives the scene as 'busy' or 'chaotic.'
This is why 'just add animation everywhere' reliably produces bad results — it overloads the motion tracking system.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Count the maximum number of simultaneous motion groups in any frame of the animation. If it exceeds 3, reduce. If the user is also expected to read text during the animation, cap at 1-2. Stagger is the primary tool for reducing simultaneous density without eliminating motion entirely.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Intentional stillness after a key visual beat is not 'wasted time.' It is the reading window — the period during which information transfers from the screen to the viewer.
logic: Motion attracts attention; stillness allows comprehension. Both are necessary.
When animation flows continuously without holds, the viewer is always in 'tracking' mode, never in 'understanding' mode.
AI-generated animation is particularly prone to hold neglect because agents treat 'continuous engagement' as a quality metric, when in fact it prevents comprehension.
The optimal hold duration after a key visual beat is 500-1500ms, depending on information density. Denser information needs longer holds.
reasoning_chains.so_what: For every key visual beat (headline, chart reveal, CTA appearance, key insight), insert an explicit hold of at least 500ms before the next motion begins. During the hold, nothing moves. Review the animation with sound off — can you read and understand each beat before the next one starts? If not, increase the hold.
